corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: While the Tagspace model is described using convolutions, we consider the linear version, which achieves comparable performance but is much faster.
This architecture is similar to the cbow model of , where the middle word is replaced by a label.
Then, we evaluate its capacity to scale to large output space on a tag prediction dataset.
Overall our accuracy is slightly better than char-CNN and char-CRNN and, a bit worse than VDCNN.
Note that we can increase the accuracy slightly by using more n-grams, for example with trigrams, the performance on Sogou goes up to 97.1%.
Unlike Tang et al. (2015), fastText does not use pre-trained word embeddings, which can be explained the 1% difference in accuracy.
Table 4: Examples from the validation set of YFCC100M dataset obtained with fastText with 200 hidden units and bigrams.
We show a few correct and incorrect tag predictions. up compared to neural network based methods increases with the size of the dataset, going up to at least a 15,000× speed-up.
To test scalability of our approach, further evaluation is carried on the YFCC100M dataset (Thomee et al., 2016) which consists of almost 100M images with captions, titles and tags.
We will release a script that recreates this dataset so that our numbers could be reproduced.
Both models achieve a similar performance with a small hidden layer, but adding bigrams gives us a significant boost in accuracy.
Our experiments show that our fast text classifier fastText is often on par with deep learning classifiers in terms of accuracy, and many orders of magnitude faster for training and evaluation.
They also have the potential to scale to very large corpus .
In this work, we explore ways to scale these baselines to very large corpus with a large output space, in the context of text classification.

Inspired by the recent work in efficient word representation learning (Mikolov et al., 2013; Levy et al., 2015), we show that linear models with a rank constraint and a fast loss approximation can train on a billion words within ten minutes, while achieving performance on par with the state-of-the-art.
In this work, we propose a simple baseline method for text classification.
We present the results in Figure .

downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
